video_title: Inside the World's BEST Airline - Singapore Airlines
author: @gk-zl1xp
text:
Hello Sam,
After viewing this video of Singapore Airlines hospitality training, i find it really basic, compared to service in 5 star and 6 star hotels. Attention to detail, smile, placement of all mise - en place on the tray table is all fine, but super BASIC for a masters degree holder hotel management student like me to watch. Singapore airline is honestly really hyped after watching this video, it gives the viewer a feeling that this is 1st year hotel management training given to the stewardesses. Maybe Singapore airlines should consider hiring hotel management graduate students who will give these "good looking stewardesses" a run for their money. Again, absolutely BASIC training. Nothing wow by Singapore airlines, sorry to say!

author: @lynkuizon5900
text: I was flying with Singapore Airlines last August of this year sitting in Economy Premium. I lost my money during the flight. I noticed it when I get home in the Philippines. My fault was leaving my Bag to go to the toilet during the flight. I have been a frequent singapore airlines flyer and it was my first time to happen this bad news. Airlines have no CCTV ( CloseCamera TV) inside the plane. I used to fly home 1 - 2x a year but only this time that it happened. I was sitting alone in the middle with 4 seats just near the Toilet. I am sure if I found my money missing before we landed, that money might be flushed in the toilet. My money was placed in the plastic and THE PLASTIC was empty when i got home  when i was about to put the money in the safe at home.  I AM INCLUDING THE PERSON WHO GOT THE MONEY IN MY DAILY PRAYERS THAT HE OR SHE WILL BE IN GOOD HEALTH  AND NEVER DO IT AGAIN TO THE PASSENGERS. IT IS NEVER EASY TO LOST MONEY ESPECIALLY THE HARD EARNED MONEY. NOW I AM THINGKING TO TRY ANOTHER AIRLINES FOR A CHANGE AND I HAVE MY LESSON LEARNED.

video_title: Inside the World's BEST Airline - Singapore Airlines
author: @danielkim7841
text: the level of professionalism and perfectionism is so high! the science behind how to plate the food to make it more appetizing was fascinating.  I know there is so much thoughtfulness and hard work put into this training. details like making sure the sauce stays close to the meat and how to add height and how to place everything shows how much care and effort and thought is put to get the final product out! Even how to hold the tray while holding the water is a detail that was important.  the customer does not know why the final product looks more professional and appetizing but they can subconsciously sense it,  due to all the ideas behind the presentation.  it is something people appreciate but cannot understand why and why Singapore airlines always seems ahead of other brands.  this video was simply fascinating with how hard the staff works! PS to be honest this video stressed me out because how high the bar is set for the staff.  if I was trying to serve a hot soup I would be worried the liner would be missing between the plate and the boiling hot soup,  and also you have to present everything in a quick minute to avoid the food getting cold.  there is a lot of variables to consider and to do this consistently everyday shows how elite the staff is. I could see why the interview process for the flight attendants is so notoriously selective because the average person could not juggle all these constraints while remaining  calm and courteous.

ok so for some reason you just haven't managed to visit romania yet so here is a list of reasons why it has to be one of the last few countires you visit!!:
1.) The amazing artwork and architecture! Romania, specifically Buchuresti, has been called little Paris so many times (Luckily it's much cheaper!!!) There are also street art tours where you can get a better understanding of graffiti!!
2.) Speaking of tours....  there are *drumroll pleaseee* FOOD TOURS!!! With a food tour you get to talk to a local and try various foods. Romania has suchhh good food and all tourists love it! They even have more traditional foods in Brasov, the country side.
3.) Speaking of Brasov, the land is GORGEOUS!! The mountains are so beautiful. THere is also a place you might have heard of... Transylvania! Where you can visit the castle that inspired Dracula. You can even visit his real house!!

text:
Sorry this is so long but this is my experience in economy class with 3 flights taking over 40 hours to get to my destination... 

I always fly economy as I don’t have a lot of money 💁🏼‍♂️ 
When I flew from Edinburgh to Melbourne via Charles de Gaulle  (CDG) and Changi and from Edinburgh to Charles de Gaulle I got orange juice and a croissant, then from CDG to changi I got breakfast, lunch and dinner unlimited drinks and snacks (15 hour flight) from changi to Melbourne I got supper and breakfast with unlimited snacks and drinks and also after each meal the gave us ice cream or an ice cream bar

text: A question, from the point of view of an Aussie who can't get to a lot of the world without spending around 20 hours in the air ... in your experience, is it better to spend 19 hours in the air all in one go & get it over-and-done-with, or is it better to have an 8hr leg followed by a leg-stretch in an airport (often for 3 to 5 hours) followed by a 12hr leg?  The total time is shorter if it's one single flight, and you don't get any time to acclimatise or sleep in that 3hr-5hr airport wait ... but on the other hand, you do get to walk around, and the constant low-pitch roar isn't there in the airport either.

author: @BDFTP69420
text: What are your thoughts on Qantas' new Project Sunrise routes from NYC/LHR-SYD, specifically them somehow planning to operate four cabins on 50% more seats than the SQ A359? I personally think anyone considering that flight in economy could easily be lured to other carriers offering one-stop service, that segment is more sensitive to price over a a few extra hours on a 20+ hour flight. From LHR - BA, QR, EY, EK, SQ. NYC has the big three plus Air Canada from EWR, and they'll be competing with their DFW/ORD/SFO/LAX offerings via their oneworld partner, American. You have to figure too that for some people a break of a couple of hours would be preferable to being stuck in economy for 20+ hours, they'll simply refuse to consider a single flight that long.

author: @BDFTP69420
text: If an award redemption or reasonable cash fare for F/J isn't available I would much prefer a layover of a couple hours at a place like DOH, DXB, AUH, SIN, etc. And given the significantly decreased passenger capacity and higher operating costs I see QF's future economy non-stops to London and NYC being very expensive, probably double the myriad of competing one-stop options. Just after a cursory Google Flights search, the current LHR-PER non-stop (in economy) comes at a 15-30% premium (or even more) versus one-stop itineraries that are between 2-4 hours longer. That flight is a standard 787-9 clocking in at 17 hours and doesn't take a capacity penalty. And at the upper end of an aircraft's range, the increase in cost per mile in terms of fuel consumption is not linear. So these fares on Project Sunrise are not going to be incrementally more expensive than other similarly long flights, they will be substantially higher.

author: @billlawrence1899
text: I can't think like a passenger, because I'm a retired pilot. You speak of jet lag. Think about your pilots. They are based in Singapore, a couple days after doing the non stop to New York. Of course, they are an augmented crew, which means they have a comfortable bunk in which they spend half the flight, but still crossing that many time zones does a real number on the body. Before their body clocks have readjusted back to their home time, they have to do it again. The longest flights I ever did were up to 14 hours, west coast to Hong Kong, and it took several days to recover back home. If I flew for Singapore, I would avoid that flight like the plague. Here's another thought just to make you nervous. Singapore has a great safety record. They do things right. BUT! Even the best pilots lose skill without practice. There are 4 pilots on that airplane. 2 flying, and 2 in the bunk. The captain will take one landing, One of the other 3 gets the other landing, and everyone else loses currency and has to get their landings in a simulator because they never get one in an airplane.

video_title: World's LONGEST Flight In Singapore A350-900ULR Business Class
author: @Breakups2Makeup
text: @lauvasquez8030 Great question! In this particular instance, I was flying so much in the year that I was able to accumulate gold status with Air Canada/Star Alliance and I guess with that particular status, you get upgraded if there's additional room in business class. In this particular situation, it was a complete surprise for me as I had just arrived from a connecting flight and was literally running to the gate. When I got there, one of the Air Canada associates pulled me aside and said the boarding pass I had was invalid and that she had a "better ticket" for me. She handed me a business class seat and I cried before hugging her lol I've done that long haul flight numerous times in economy and let me tell you, flying home in business was a godsend. When I didn't have status, I would often just ask the gate attendees if there were any upgrades available and sometimes, you just get lucky, other times, you get a firm no. It never hurts to ask and to be kind to everyone you speak to! :) Hope this helps!

author: @VDHFilms
text:
I still remember flying on this route for the very first time in the mid-2000s, when they operated the A340-500. It was my first time flying SQ's business class (at the time they called it 'Raffles Class') and my first time in business class in general. Boy, it was an experience I will never forget! And on the return flight, I practically begged my parents to buy a model of the A340 for me~

I finally got to try it again back in summer 2019 in premium economy. Still great as ever, probably better than any of the SQ flights I had. I definitely want to try it again, this time on their latest business class! It really brought back a lot of memories of this flight and my trip to Singapore~!

text:
I used to sit in East Coast Park (in Singapore) looking out at the waiting container ships in the ocean, and observe the SQ flights taking off from Changi for the last 2 years, and ponder who was onboard and their reasons for travel during this pandemic, wishing I was sitting onboard too. Fortunate enough to take several flights during pandemic too. 

It is so good to be back onboard and hear that stirring music from the safety video - really brings a tear to the eye. It's a feeling of home (for now as a foreigner here) but also sanctuary as you feel so safe and warm while on SQ. Just a wonderful experience.

text: From what I know, amenity kits are only brought up from SIN, so that number of amenity kits have to be able to last 2 sectors, back and forth. So it is understandable that they don't give it out readily when flying out of SIN as they have to conserve for the returning sector. However, if you really need that amenity kit, feel free to ask and they will give it to you if there is enough. Do also note that they don't cater for every passengers for both sectors as they have to consider space and weight constraints.

author: @clementrono1478
text:
You used up the entire runway because you had approximately 111 tonnes of fuel onboard. It is cheaper for Singapore Airlines to carry that much fuel, with fewer passengers than to have 2 takeoffs. 
Technically, there are three routes from Singapore to Newark. The 19 hour is the shortest and the most convenient many times of the year. The choice of routing is influenced by levels of cosmic radiation, weather, and any ultra long haul flight like this one is shorter through the poles. Singapore and Newark are on opposite sides of each other.... such like matters. Also, interestingly, is the fact that this flight passes through a path almost directly above the North Pole. At about 87 degrees, to be precise. The Captain will always come on -on P.A to announce that.

author: @RSK-GamerZ
text: The Worlds Longest Flight is technically from New York (JFK) to Singapore directly, operated by the same airline using the same aircraft. It beats this one by just 3 miles (5km). They actually confirmed the JFK one is longer than the Newark one. They measured the distance of both of the flights. For the route, the route they take really depends on the time of the year, and what the winds are doing, and they choose the most optimum route to fly to Singapore. When I flew it, the route I took was the polar one, which takes you over the top of the world. The official confirmation of the JFK one was at a total distance of 15,349 km ((9,537 miles) and the Newark one was confirmed at a total distance of 15,344 km ((9,534 miles). So I'm afraid you are wrong, the JFK flight is actually longer than the Newark one. But it really depends on the route, and there are 3 of them. Usually they more commonly fly the one that takes you over the top of the world, past the north pole, all the way back down to Singapore. And if you fly that route, the JFK one is longer. If you fly the route that takes you across the pacific ocean, the JFK one is longer. But for the route you took, the one that takes you over the Atlantic ocean, the Newark one might be slightly longer. Recently they may have been flying that route, but generally, out of the 3 routes, the JFK flight beats the Newark flight 2-1. So therefore the JFK one is generally longer, hands down.

author: @kevinang5209
text: SQ clearly cut too many corners, particularly in terms of food and lounge service. Cathay Pacific, Japanese, and Middle Eastern airlines appear to perform significantly better in this regard. Like on the flight, the food in their flagship lounge at Changi lacks both variety and quality. It's like they deliberately chose low-cost ingredients. Furthermore, I find their seats to be impractical. Changing the seat position became a chore, and the foot well was too small unless you got the bulkhead seat which is not easy to score one. I much prefer the Apex suite, herringbone seats, and even staggered seats to the ones offered by SQ. And yes, the non-touch screen also perplexed me as well.

author: @rylary
text: Just did this flight last week from JFK to SIN in premium economy and was stuck in the middle. I thought it was going to be really bad, but it was actually not as bad as I had imagined. The seat configuration was a little different from your flight, it was a 2-4-2. I do agree with you though, that jetlag was a b**** but also, my biggest gripe was the meal service. We had 2 full meals and snacks inbetween on demand. For such a long flight, they should have given us at least one more meal! I was starving SIN to JFK and from JFK to SIN. In contrast, SIN to LHR, we get 2 meals too. Since this flight is way longer, they should have really done something about it. Great video, love fortune centre too!

video_title: Flying Singapore Airlines Premium Economy Singapore to Christchurch ✈️
author: @thisunchartedworld
text: It really depends on how much you value leg room and seat width. I wouldn't upgrade if you can handle being in economy for a long time but it made a huge difference in terms of confidence and the flight went a LOT faster for us. It's one of the best experiences we've had on a plane but if I had to choose between the seat and an extra $3500 to spend in Europe, I'd probably spend the money in Europe! If you have any health or pain issues then the seat probably is worth it though. It depends on your personal circumstances.

author: @macsanta
text:
Accept the baggage delivery responsibility and do deliver my baggage.

Do you have any update ? We travelled in Air India from Singapore(Singapore Airlines allocated this) and you people delivered the bags to TRV through scoot. Nobody(SIA/Scoot/Air India) is taking responsibility to deliver the baggage. This is causing so much trouble. Can you deliever it?


== main story ====

Our flight got delayed from SFO and there were multiple issues with our travel through Singapore Airlines.
We were users of Singapore Airlines for last 10 years but this travel made us to look for other alternatives.
The travel experiance was very BAD and we received very BAD experiance from singapore airlines and with singapore
immigration. Part of the problem with Singapore Immigration is related to the Singapore Airline too. 

Because of the airline delay on Sunday (20th Nov 2022), we had to miss our connecting flight to TRV. There were
multiple issues with handling of this delayed issue. 

1. Singapore Airlines didnt give guarantee letter to us. After 16 hours travel from SFO, we were
   made to wait in the immigration for more than 90 minutes. The issue is, Singapore Airlines didn't
   provide the right documents for us (Guarantee letters)
  
2. The hotels provided were small enough for four people. We were given two adult bed rooms and i am
   not sure how 4 people can stay there. The rooms provided were not comfortable.
   
3. Singapore Airlines didn't arrange for food in the hotel.

4. We were not given good connecting flights to India. We had to miss a day in our life because of
   Singapore Airlines and extra 9 hours of travel time and lot of mess up.
   
5. We were given Air India flights but Air India didnt get any information about it.
   They didnt had any clue about our checkedin bags. We spent 2 hours in the Air India counter
   to sort out our baggage related issues.
   
6. We didnt receive our baggages yet. I blame Singapore Airlines for it. They should have provided
   boarding passes and guarante letters.

7. You transfered all our problems to Air India. Worst of all, we dont know when do we get the baggages
   from Air India.

Singapore Airlines provided the worst travel experiance this time and i will remember this for my life.
We had very bad experiance with the best airline and best airport. All in all, the worst of worst experiance.

I take pride in sharing this experiance in public. 

I know flight delays are common. I know Emirates is best in handling this kind of issues. Singapore and 
Singpaore Airline dont have any clue about how to handle it better.

VERY BAD. Is there any better word to explain this ?
Thanks for your Worst Service.

author: @PapaVanTwee5
text: I've only flown Singapore once, on a 777 from ORD-AMS-ORD. It was my favorite of the 4 I did by myself while my now wife was still living in the Netherlands in the ealry/mid 2000s. United and US Air were also 777s, and KLM was a 747. While it was great to check off the 747, KLM was the worst, as the food wasn't so much better than United and US Air, and it didn't have in seat entertainment. United and US Air were so similar, I'd have them tie at 2nd, but US Air did have a 4 hour delay due to a toilet that refused to be fixed, and we needed a replacement plane.  Singapore had the best food, the best IFE, and the best flight attendants (and they weren't bad looking, either.) I'd take 19 hours in premium economy for the experience.

author: @brunoribeiroprofeta1664
text:
Hi there, hope you're having a terrific week! :)

I have been trying to talk to a human being, but it seems impossible, so I thought of trying it here:

Singapore airlines deliberately decided not to load my vessel with my suitcase and gave me no heads up in regards to this. When I arrived at my final destination - after 2 other stops - , I didn't see it, went to the baggage claim desk and was told that the aircraft was too heavy to carry my suitcase - I never even conceived that an airline could actively simply decide that, without any communication or negotiation, they could just not load your luggage and leave you waiting for it, powerlessly, at the airport; and then have you go through all the stress of chasing it for days. They opened a ticket to track my suitcase and many emails have been sent saying that it hasn't been found yet. Their Brisbane office does not answer the only phone number provided to me. 

Sydney's office took literally one hour to answer my call and said it was able to find it and assign a flight to it, which should arrive tonight - 2 full days after my arrival -. I have WET clothes in there that are being damaged and damaging other goods while they don't send the suitcase, and other important articles for my everyday use, like clothes and leather shoes to go to work. I'm having to work from home as a result, against my company's policy. Although Sydney said there was a flight assigned to my suitcase, I kept receiving emails saying the same suitcase wasn't located yet, and that they "were doing their best" to find it, so I also had contradictory information from your team.
I wasn't given a final date to have this resolved, nor any sort of compensation offer from the airline. This is very poor customer handling and has ruined that entire Singapore Airlines experience, which already hadn't had any stand-outs. 

Is anyone here able to help me out (for real) with this?

Thank you!

video_title: What Singapore Airlines’ First, Business, And Economy Classes Are Like | Business Insider
author: @shallbee.
text:
I could never understand why such "segregation" exists and thrives in the air travel business. Some passengers are squeezed and jammed together to the limits while others can take a nap in horizontal position and take a shower. To me this sounds completely anti-human and I will never fly 1st class regardless of my income level, I just wouldn't be comfortable to enjoy that luxury knowing that somebody on the same plane can't even straighten their legs. 
Of course inequality exists and is inevitable due to human nature, but I truly believe that certain public sectors must be regulated by government. Like what the heck, air travel is essential in the modern world and therefore should be treated as a form of public transportation, the same way busses are. There's no luxury seats on the bus, right? But there are priority seats for people with disabilities, then why planes should be different? Why wouldn't everybody on a plane squeeze equally which would allow everyone travel with the same level of comfort. There must be priority seats though for people who need it either due to health reasons or just specifics of their body, like if they're too tall to fit into a regular seat with the same comfort as a shorter person can, things like that.
And there can be premium flights where all seats are premium, for people who really have money for that and they want some extra luxury for themselves, like sure, that's fair... as I told not all people are equal no matter how fair or unfair that is. But on a regular flights somebody's comfort should not be at the cost of someone's discomfort.

author: @hotspot930
text: This is what I liked to Singapore Airlines, most of their attendants whether what class of seats you are. Attendants are always helpful to their passengers even if you missed your meals (if you’re at sleep at the time of meals distribution) the attendant will get back to you once you’re awake and offered you your meals. Always checking their passengers. While British Airways, Flight Attendants are not helpful at all. During meal time, if you’re not going to ask for water they’ll not give you water, if you missed your meal, they will not get back to you. The worst in-flight service I experienced. I also felt discriminated at the time of checked-in, the ground attendant told me that there’s no seat available to the flight that I chose. I was surprised and told right away to the ground attendant, I need to be in that flight because I do have work the next day. I was angry telling to the ground attendant, if you’re telling me that I will be in the next flight then the airline will pay me then because I will be missing my work the next day. I told to the ground attendant, by the way I’m a Scientist and pay me per hour if you’re going to put me in the next flight. Then the ground attendant right away told me, see me at the boarding gate sir. And I responded, “…ok thank you!” Then I got her name. Then she gave me at the end a seat for the flight to San Francisco (by the way, I booked this three months ago). Awful service, I should say!

video_title: What Singapore Airlines’ First, Business, And Economy Classes Are Like | Business Insider
author: @BigBoiiLeem
text:
Not necessarily in general, but Asia does have some of the nicest airlines out there. Although the Middle Eastern carriers among others aren't too far behind in terms of quality. For instance, I flew to Vietnam from Auckland, Malaysia Airlines from Auckland to Kuala Lumpur, Malaysia from Kuala Lumpur to Ho Chi Minh, and then Vietnam Airlines from Ho Chi Minh to Hue. 

The first flight was... okay. My seat didn't recline, the other passengers were rude and loud, the flight attendants were nice but you could never get their attention. The second flight was awful, the plane was dirty, I couldn't see out my window because it was covered in schmutz, as was my tray table, and the flight attendants were pleasant but completely absent except for one drinks and meals round. That experience means I will never fly Malaysian again. 

Then, we flew Vietnam Airlines... and it was amazing. Granted we did have to take a bus to get to the plane on the tarmac, but once we were on it, it was cool and the air had a bit of mist in it, the flight attendants were amazing and attentive, and they were always looking to be helpful. I ruptured my eardrum on the previous flight and we couldn't get any help at all, and then on the Vietnam flight, the flight attendants were regularly checking in to see if we needed anything or if there was anything they could do. If I ever get the chance to fly Vietnam Airlines again, I will jump at that chance. 

So there is a variety, but there is certainly some star players.

This video is not fair and wrongl idealize flying and here is the reason why!
- the Paris agreement is set at 1.5 Celsius bc in excess of the amount of CO2 that leads to 1.5Celsius global warming, global warming is not reversible.
- it takes ca 15,000 for CO2 to disappear naturally in the atmosphere.
- in order to hold the Paris agreement, we need to reduce our individual CO2 emission by ca 7% per annum, which is equivalent to the reduction derived by the COVID pandemic. We need to achieve this every year until 2050.
- While civil aviation is not the largest source of CO2, it heavily relies on fossil fuel. And in many cases it is not necessary, neither to enjoy the sun for tourist nor for business people to attend BS meetings.
…SO STOP IDEALIZING long haul business/first class flying! This is not responsible, unless you have been paid by Singapore to promote this climate negative activity!

author: @AdamLop314
text: 7:50 lol this is hilarious that the journalist actually brought a damn tape measurer. You measure diagonally, not horizontally. No reputable manufacturer classifies their screens horizontally. So if a Monitor or TV says it's 32-inches wide, it's actually 32-inches wide diagonally from the top left corner to the bottom right (and vice versa). You also are only (usually) supposed to measure the actual screen/panel, not including bezels or dead-space but that one is more 50/50.

author: @markiobook8639
text:
I am a frequent traveller, and I have thousands of air miles from Singapore membership. Singapore is a GOOD airline that USED TO BE singularly fantastic. But they got complacent hence this kind of "high quality journalistic article" now Singapore Air is nothing like this at all. Singapore has gone all out to depict itself UNREALISTICALLY here
I fly the Asia-London flight at least 4 times a year. First class is nothing like this at all- this is only some select flights. Attendants have very poor English skills (Singlish), often rude & incompetent airline hostesses and to be honest a lot of unprofessional ones (flirting with the male customers even in front of their wives)- I have switched to one of the small Gulf state airlines. These have been far more professional, better facilities, in cases far better airports than Changi.

text: Here is my thoughts about the airline business no matter what country they belong to:  “Class” treatment is fundamentally wrong.  When we all strive for freedom and equal treatment , the last thing I need is being treated like I am some kind of a loser and therefore, I must sit in the back of the plane because I can’t afford it or unwilling to pay for it… Every passenger should be treated in a dignified manner with respect and curtesy… All passengers should have the same first class like seating arrangements… Same access to food service.  I cannot think of any other business or industry where people are given a “class” treatment.  There are not that many out there.  Cruises, train rides to name a few… I will condemn this way of doing business forever…

I highly suggest doing Book the Cook option under manage booking for your Business class flight. Do it a few days before your flight. You can choose from a few different chefs personal menus. It's much better than the in-flight menu in my opinion. For instance my last flight I got lamb shank and lobster tail that was fantastically prepared.
I'm glad you got a bulk head business seat on your 2nd flight. It has the most leg room of all seats. So wide! Singapore Airlines service is phenomenal. I don't think I'll fly anything else again.

text:
Some of the journalist comments make me who can only afford economy ticket singair feel so low.

She is taking premium economy and worrying if she is be able to sleep, given the seat is wider and more inclined? I dont know for those who take economy ticket like me, how should I feel? no sleep at all?

Then for the business class, she mentioned at the end that ' we've made it' and ' honestly it really wasnt that bad in business class at all' (11:49)? Was she expecting flying business class such a bad experience? 

Since singair services standard is high (best airline in the world) , their services really pay off so these comments annoy me to a certain degree. I will be definitely overjoyed and happy to take the premium economy with no complaints.

Or perhaps I've missed a point here?

author: @mummymantras
text:
A few months back, I flew back to Singapore from London via SQ Business Class, and I was so exhausted after my long worktrip that I just wanted to sleep the moment I finished dinner. I asked the stewardess for more pillows while she was making my bed (I just like to cradle a lot of pillows while I sleep) but I fell asleep the moment I lay down, before she could return with more pillows! But when I woke up a few hours later, I was surrounded by a huge pile of fluffy pillows. It was like sleeping in a nest! 😂
It was still the best sleep I've ever gotten on an airplane!

author: @SusieQSydney
text:
For a "Business Insider" if you are you don't seem to be too culturally minded or well researched. "The rice soup" has a name it's call congee.
         Well, it might seem like a simple dish to you, it's gravitas is that it's subtle flavours and recipe is only created one low heat an extensive preparation sometimes upwards of an hour slow boiling time so you can oversimplify by calling it prawn rice soup.

And if that beef noodle soup seemed to taste great has nothing to do with your taste buds. It looks to me like it influenced by Vietnamese national dish Pho which complex flavour you can't seem to distinguish from any other bowl of Asian noodle soup has no less than a dozen spices and aromatics and rather expensive bone broth of the more expensive beef cuts and chicken as well an a preparation time of an hour to of days depending on how complexity and or nuance the chef should want to make it so it taste good at any altitude.

This review is becoming less reflective of Singapore Airline standards compared as it's Business Insider's reviewer's journalistic calibre in taste.

And it shouldn't matter too much that you speak with a condescending self-entitled Western tone it's common and typical enough to hear from every xenophobe and their dog from American writing vlogs like they know know a thing or 2 about taste or standards ironically which can either the make or break a company who never ellude to the fact that their taste which is rather Individual and therefore subjective which is all relative I guess.

        As even a stupid opinion is an opinion but the expression "Business Insider" projects an image of sophisticate with standards who would do extensive and a great deal of due diligence before speaking publically on any topic let alone that of travel - rather then a lazy unresearched intern to world of hospitality and travel in general pretending she knows something by being a hospitality and travel connoisseur and snob.

author: @summerjung6418
text: Real talk, on a flight from Dubai to America there happened to be a seat free between me and this random dude. He actually switched seats with his coworker who was assigned that seat to sit near me I guess. So, he begins telling me about his wife and kids. He then offers me wine - I decline. He asks if I want to listen to his music. I’m embarrassed to say I reluctantly said sure to the music. Anyways, after a while I lay my head down in the empty seat (long journey - was actually traveling home from Africa, Dubai was layover) and he actually bent down, just like in this video, and GAVE ME A KISS ON MY HEAD. I was like, guess it’s time to sit up. Very uncomfortable. I’ve grown a lot personally since then and would NEVER be silently uncomfortable again - would definitely say something in the future 😅

video_title: How to make premium economy better than first class #airline #flight
author: @kevincorrales9774
text:
Also had this happen to me once😂. 

I was 19 flying from Boston to Buffalo with a layover in Charlotte. During the first leg of the trip I placed in the middle seat between two woman, one middle aged (window seat) and another college aged (aisle seat).  For reference I’m a pretty big dude 6’1 @ 220lbs. This being said I didn’t wanna take up too much space. 

Despite trying to fight it, I fell asleep as I had just smoked a joint before getting to the airport 😅. When I woke up the college aged girl was on my shoulder and I was leaning on her head. We were both asleep. 

As comfortable as it was I was caught off guard and didn’t want to seem like a creep so I jolted up out of shock and woke her up and began apologizing 😭😭

She was totally fine with what had happened but was more so laughing at the fact I was profusely apologizing for something that wasn’t a big deal. 😂

After that I got up to go to the bathroom and so I had to ask her to get up momentarily so I could go. 

But I fell like 3 times while trying to get out of my seat and into the aisle 🤣🤣

I think the paranoia of being high on an airplane made me much more self conscious (and clumsy) than I normally am 😭😭🤣. 

Long story short everything was normal at the end of the flight and now I have a funny story about embarrassing myself on an airplane :).

I had a similar experience 20 years ago on a bus ride back from church camp. I ended up seated next to a girl I hadn’t met before and she was so pretty I couldn’t speak. It took 15 year old me an hour to work up the courage to have a conversation with her. She ended up falling asleep on my lap and I didn’t dare fall asleep because I didn’t want to move and wake her up.

We got back home the next morning and after some more chit chat she wrote her number on my hand. I was so excited and when I got home I realized the ink had smudged so much I couldn’t read it.

I’m happily married now with three kids. Kylie, if you’re out there, I hope life has been good to you too.

text:
Emirates, for $23,000 without 🚿 in first class? Come see what we have to offer! 
The special style of uniform Singapore and Malaysian airlines are from their subculture called NYONYA, form fitting long skirts and 3/4 sleeves fitted blouse. I wonder how practical it is during emergency when they have to save lifes?
My first A380 economy class from Hong Kong to Paris was accidental, i booked it without realizing it was an A380. I set 2 rows behind business class, i saw the spiral stairs up to first class lounge, there was a cleaning lady wearing different uniform just to tidy the lounge area! 
The economy toilet seat has "wood" trimming. I can put my backpack in between my seat and the window 🪟!
If you have a 5 hours ir more layover in Dubai, your boarding pass get you to the all you can eat  Emirates restaurant inside Dubai airport.
The window shades is not unique to Singapore's airline,
Norwegian and Turkish airlines has it.

text: I‘d always heard that SA were in the top 3 for business class travel, but watching this video would really put me off trying. No menus, no ability to eat at times of your own choosing, awful, unappetising food (OK - I get this may be partly down to US point of embarkation, but ultimately airline has to take accountability for choice of it‘s vendors). No PJs on such a long flight. No overnight bag if you dont ask for it (if you are not a regular traveller - you probably wouldn‘t ask). Given all of above, I think I‘d even choose BA over this offering - and I am no BA fan! Incredibly disappointing for such a long flight 😢

author: @adriansim5940
text: It is certainly very impressive and I really envy your experience.  However, I have to say this to Singapore Airlines and to Qatar and Emirates (these 3 are probably the worst)....what an absolute WASTE of resources!! The First Class lounge appeared devoid of human souls...I was wondering who the chefs were cheffing for? it looked like you are the only one flying Suites for a short 4 HOUR flight!! Who would fly these suites except Government Officials, Senior airline executives and MAYBE, MAYBE one or two uber rich to snap some photos to show their mates? While the rest of humanity squeeze into the cattle stocks below. I can understand the expense spent on business class passengers but cannot seriously commercially justify Suite Cabins except as an airline executive's vanity project. . Have you ever cynically reviewed airline commercials...they only advertise the products (ie First and Business Class) that 95% of their passengers don't get to use or have the ability to purchase! The reality is that of small, uncomfortable seats, noisy cabins, poor quality food that is a galaxy away from their commercials. Ya, now go ahead...crucify me!!

author: @michaelyip5290
text: Great to see this trip report on video!  I’ve taken this itinerary LAX-NRT-SIN four times past couple years versus the direct flight, specifically for the same reason you did… for the extra meals, flight time and the ANA lounge in NRT 🤣.   Relatively short layover as you noticed… but walking brisk… I can get to the open and spacious larger ANA lounge by gate 52 in less than 10 minutes from gate 35.  It’s well worth the walk for the relaxed seat by the windows with hot ramen and curry rice with chicken karaage on top!  A good half hour in lounge and still make it back before priority boarding.

author: @acl6872
text:
Fellow Perthite who begrudges the cost of flights. I usually fly Qatar business out of Perth to Europe and was happy to swallow $6k pre-covid but now it's around $9k.
Now, I used Finnair and book my flights out of Bali to Europe, through codeshare you fly Qatar (Qsuites) for a fraction of the cost. My last trip to Europe , business was AUD$3900. You just need to get to Bali, if you have Qantas points it doesn't cost much on Jetstar and you can bookend your trip with a couple of days in Bali!

text:
Just flew on the B777 and the A380.  Both were awkward.  

On the B777 you had to get up and flip the seat over to get the lie-flat.  Once it was down it wasn't too bad.

I HATED the A380 arrangement.  Feet off to the side in a cubby and an awkward footrest.  Had a terrible sleep.  

The food and service was good on both, but MUCH more personal and attentive on the B777.

Let him out and then close the door.
Just kidding. While it is very frightening, airplane doors cannot be opened in mid air because of the air pressure, unless the plane is relatively close to ground (interior and exterior air pressure difference is not too great). 
I know some people said why other passengers are not helping. If that person is able to open the door, anyone who is not strapped down will be sucked out of the plane. It is a tough call.

video_title: World's LONGEST Flight In Singapore A350-900ULR Business Class
author: @KeithHays-ek4vr
text: I was airline crew for 28 years. Did you notice that he ate when he was hungry, yet tried to outsmart his body clock? - Everyone is different, I guess, however I believe for myself that sleep is like food. - It's a basic physiological need. I eat when I'm hungry, and sleep when I'm tired. I'm not a businessman, and won't be working at my destination. I usually go straight to my hotel after the flight, rest, and sleep if I want to, then head out for dinner later. My golden rule is to avoid alcohol and caffeine when I fly. They'll both make me go to the bathroom more often, and dehydrate me. I drink sufficient water, some tea, and the fruits and juices out of Singapore are lovely. Altitude also exacerbates the effect of alcohol on the body and mind, and consequently aggravates the effects of jetlag. - I would advise enjoying a Singapore Sling or two after you've arrived at your destination. For me, it's a poor tradeoff to drink alcohol on a flight, deprive myself of adequate sleep, then suffer for it after I arrive at my destination. Fly well, everyone! ✈️

author: @jordillach3222
text:
_"For my own life,I must do everything I need to stop that guy."_
You don't need to. The pressure differential makes it impossible for a person to open the door mid-flight.

video_title: Passenger tries to open airplane door on Korean Air A330 #airbus #aviation #turbulence
author: @jordillach3222
text:
@kingosaitoh789 _"It's the opposite. The pressure in the cabin is too high, so it opens."_
No, because airplane doors are of the plug type. The door is larger than the perimeter of its frame, exactly the same as in a car, but in the oposite direction.
That is why when opening an aircraft  door, the crew members first move it inside a little, then rotate it slightly so it can pass through the frame opening and only then they move  it out.
Inside air pressure keeps the door firmly against the frame in-flight.

author: @pacmanc8103
text: Many/most of these business class pods have large protrusions where the footwells of pods behind encroach on the pods in front.  This is fine if the seat in the pod in front is along the the aisle; however, if the seat is against the window and in lie-flat position, it is almost impossible to get out to use the toilet.  Imagine getting out of your bed with a wall alongside you from your head to below your knees.  I specifically avoid window seats in business class for this reason (I have never found this an issue in first class because the pods are quite different).  I totally agree that Turkish business class is miserable and even worse with windowside seats.  The ONLY semi-comfortable seats are the first row where the footwells are wide and deep.

video_title: The 10 Best and Worst Business Class Seat 2025
author: @PassportBrosBusinessClass
text:
I am close to 6'7" tall.
I can ONLY fly business class at this point because I absolutely need the space. 
I typically fly to Dubai or I fly to Southeast Asia.

MY FAVORITE AIRCRAFT IS THE EMIRATES A380-800.  The Asiana A380 isn't as perfect.   From NYC, that means 13 hours from JFK to Dubai - followed by 4 -6 hours to Seychelles, Maldives or Thailand.    EMIRATES has been my go to for this travel destination. Their seats are like a Mercedes Maybach (by design).  The best seat is next to the window (if you're alone) or  buying both middle seats if you're with your wife/gf. The seats closest to each other being the best. I've seen some couples who couldn't secure both seats next to each other forced to be apart for the flight.    WORSE: are the seats near the galley/bathroom.  Too much light, too much noise and too much traffic.   You want to be 1-2 rows ahead of the galley. 

EMIRATES lounge in Dubai is like heaven on Earth.  The food is fantastic. The selection is varied.  The alcohol selection is perfect (I drown myself in MOET).   When I get there I sign up for the shower and take a long relaxing shower so that I am fresh for the next flight.  The business class seat isn't private (unless you get the seat closest to the window), but the lounge offers decent quiet areas for privacy when it isn't crowded. 


My next favorite is the A350.  It's a 16 hour nonstop flight from JFK to Manila (I've taken twice) in business class.  Their food is good.  Their alcohol selection is good.  Their Paggs lounge is good - especially when flying early in the morning and not having had breakfast.   The business class seat is wide, spacious and I can relax, BUT I can't lay flat - I'm too tall. 

My LEAST FAVORITE is the Boeing  777.  HOWEVER,  I flew ANA and I really liked their "THE ROOM" product.
#1 I didn't really like the Japanese food much.  
#2 Their alcohol and sake choices were great, but I'm more of a champagne person.
#3 The ROOM's closing doors make it just as private as Emirate's 1st class. 
#4 The ANA lounge has a great bathroom but the lounge area offers no privacy.

video_title: The 10 Best and Worst Business Class Seat 2025
author: @PassportBrosBusinessClass
text:
This summer I flew ANA's "THE ROOM" from JFK to Haneda.  14 hours total. Two flights - roundtrip.   I spent the summer in Bali and Singapore. 
YES, the ROOM is a game changer.  It's doors make it private and give you a level of privacy that you normally only get in first class. 
I was able to curl up and lay nearly flat in the folded out seat.   It wasn't completely comfortable for me because of my height and size, but it worked well enough. There were a couple things I noted about it. 

#1 I didn't really like the Japanese food much.  
#2 Their alcohol and sake choices were great, but I'm more of a champagne person.
#3 The ROOM's closing doors make it just as private as Emirate's 1st class. 
#4 The ANA lounge has a great bathroom but the lounge area offers no privacy.

#5 They could save so much weight if they didn't have the pretentious "medicine cabinet" design and just used pockets instead.  The door actually got in the way of my GaN rapid charger.  Their built in USB ports aren't fast charging  (Especially in the lounge).   and you'll still need adapters for laptops or powerful smartphones.  A 100W GaN charger with multiple ports is a necessity.

author: @edeter2260
text: I have flow in business class on a few  US domestics flights (Northwest before merging with Delta, Delta, and American airlines).  but the absolute best flight I ever had was in March 1993 when I had 4 seats (middle row seats) to myself on a Continental Airlines flight from Houston to Los Angeles (that plane was a stop over from Boston).  I was able to create my own first/business class flight that included a dinner meal (US domestic flights offered them on domestic coach fare flights in the mid 1990s).  This was on the best airplane every made an flow, the legendary L1011 (I was in the 3 to last middle row on that flight).  Having 3 seats to myself on Southwest comes a very distant 2nd on a 737 come (not really hard to get).  I do like flying business class since that is usually on sale for just about every airlines when comparing to their coach fare prices with all the add on extras.  Some day I will try the middle eastern airline carriers ( which I am looking forward to but I don't believe it will ever beat my L1011 flight - but hopefully those middle eastern carriers will except my challenge).

video_title: The 10 Best and Worst Business Class Seat 2025
author: @Anthonyoutsidethebox
text:
Sam really liked this video review of all these various business classes and I’ve had the pleasure of sitting in business class on quite a few of the air carriers you mentioned. I have flown on Qatar the most in their business class mainly from DFW to Doha and a couple trips Doha to Bangkok and Doha to Miami. Have always thoroughly enjoyed their customer service and the privacy and comfort the Q-suite offers although sometimes they keep the cabin temperature too warm for my liking but that’s my personal preference. 

My second most favorite has been ANA’s The Room! You are absolutely correct about how wide that seat is and I loved that. Combined with their excellent customer service I thought it was an outstanding experience flying from Haneda to SFO  and look forward to flying with them again.

I did also fly Japan airlines but only was in Premium Economy which I thought was very comfortable but we’re talking business class so moving on….

Flew on a Turkish 787 from Istanbul to SFO direct and you were right! Those seats are NARROW. I’m 6ft tall but only 185lbs so not a huge guy but it was hard to get comfortable in that narrow seat but I did appreciate their customer service. The headset they provided was very tight and put a lot of pressure on my head so wasn’t a fan.  I did however fly business class from Houston to Istanbul on a 777 and they had that 2-3-2 configuration and like you said I did sleep very well although I did have an unfair advantage as I was on the bulkhead with no one next to me and business class was pretty empty

For me the worst  has been any US domestic carrier. They still do not understand how to provide a top notch international business class product and the only one that remotely tries is United with their Polaris but they are waaay overcharging for that product compared to any international carrier. 

Based off this video and your recent video with your girlfriend Fiona to Oman I’d like to try Oman airlines and Air France so I look forward to that one day. 

One that was not mentioned here but I had the pleasure to fly business class was Finnair DFW to Helsinki. It’s a usual 1-2-1 in business so was comfortable sitting up and the service was excellent but laying flat was not very comfortable and they did not offer a mattress pad similar to what many other carriers offer so the bed was quite firm for me. 

Thanks for another great video Sam!

author: @エラー-e7v
text:
Travelled on Qatar 787 Dreamliner from Edinburgh to Doha 2015 & 2017. it had had good BC seats - I sat 5a on first trip solo 2015, then aisle seats with family member in 2017 liked the herringbone design - decent level of privacy since the passenger is looking ahead and unless you are quite tall you can’t see the passenger in front, aisle or other side. The Airbus A320/330 (Doha-Lahore) less privacy though there was a screen divider. Loads of leg room on this flight. 

Now they are using the Airbus 350-900 (Edi-Doha) and Boeing 777-300 ER (Doha-Lahore) with QSuite for Doha-Lahore unless they change it last minute which Qatar have done in the past.

Never travelled Emirates though they have same aircraft but based on videos and stock photos it appears they don’t have the same configuration as does Qatar therefore a no no. 

Air New Zealand and Air Philippines - the less said the better

text: Have to agree with Sam regarding Apex suites. Imho, these provide the best leg comfort/room (plus privacy) as your legs don't feel like they are restricted in a tiny footwell. Also, the herringbone seats found in airlines such as China Airlines, have a low lying table, just underneath the IFE screen; and this makes turning around or sleeping on your side (in your flat bed) quite difficult. Therefore, the older business class products that are in the 2-2-2 configuration are not as bad as they're made out to be.. as the leg space is far superior.

text:
1) Calling Q Suite a business class seat is an accounting and marketing trick - it's closer to (or actually) a First Class product.
2) NH's The Room is the best. 'll confess that ANA is my all time favorite, but it's really the best Business class seat I've experienced.
3) SQ is stellar as well - I fly SQ a lot, the nonstops between SIN and LAX or EWR, and I quite enjoy the seats. I sleep sideways.

Last but not least, JL's and EY's business class products are excellent. Highly recommended. ANA and SIN: most recommended. QR: not.

author: @barnzize
text: I’ve flown in Air New Zealand’s business class seat many times, mostly on night flights. Since the cabin is usually dark, you don’t see people’s feet, and there’s no point looking out the window because it’s dark outside. The mattress topper is fantastic-better than most hotel beds! Unlike other business class seats, it doesn’t feel claustrophobic, and you’re free to turn and move around comfortably during the night. My only complaints are the small IFE screen and the limited seat storage. I really hope their new seat keeps the same fantastic mattress topper!

text:
You missed out telling us how long each of these lie-flat options is. When planning travel for a tall person with significant health issues, the lie-flat seat does need to have enough length in it for them to actually lie flat. Also, an indicator of which routes each airline flies would be really helpful - because the best seat in the world is no use at all if that airline doesn't fly that particular route.
Thank you. Despite my criticisms, this was a very useful starting point vid.

author: @MrEye4get
text: We recently flew Thai Airlines Business Class from Bangkok to Tokyo.  The outbound flight was a redeye with nearly zero service.  I was handed a small bottle of water and told good night.  No drinks, alcohol or meals other than a simple breakfast.  Disappointed.  The return flight was better as it was a daylight flight with full service.  Sadly, the seats were narrow, and the foot box was very small.  It was impossible to lay on your side!  The over-the-chest and lap seatbelts are uncomfortable and unnecessary. The staggered seating means you are right next to your neighbor or more than a meter away.  It's awkward if you fly with your family.

author: @mcnkchaw2032
text: I have been a regular passenger for SIA for the last one decade. Most of the time the experience was pleasant. However my recent flight experience with SIA has been a huge disappointment. On my 14 hours journey flight from Singapore to London, I have two English women behind asked me to adjust my seat upright outside meal time, take off and landing. I refused their request. The inflight crew who was made aware of this incident has not been fair in handling the issue and cause unnecessary chaos, stress during my journey. The air crew insisted I should sit upright throughout my journey even outside meal time.They even came to my seat and cause a scene by raising their voice in front of me and a child. The inflight crew on duty are Farham who is the team leader, he was disrespectful, rude and offensive by raising his voice in front of all passenger. Shannon Tay one of the stewardess who spread incorrect message which escalated the incident. I have reported this to complaint department but they denied the wrong doing. And we are still waiting to receive an appropriate response for your the traumatised and distressed experience given to me and a young child.

author: @Amicitas
text: Good set of reviews!  I flew Singapore Airlines from SIN to SFO a couple of months back and loved the 'weird' flip down seat. Why?  As a side sleeper it was ideal - the 'angle' sleep position was perfect, the 'bed' and bedding were soft and I had pillows to brace all around me (three they provided plus one I snagged from the SA Club in the airport). It was the best sleep I've ever had on a plane. Plus the A350 is so much quieter than its Boeing equivalent - noticeably for me as I only had to use earplugs to block the world out. I definitely recommend it!

text: Unfortunately Sam gets it totally wrong on ANA.  Yes, the seat is wide.  Yes, it has a door.  However, the seat is as hard a concrete.  Terrible for sleeping.  Second, one must sleep at an angle.  Third, there is limited recline in the seated position.  Fourth, the height of the tray table is awkward.  For eating one must be propped up with pillows.  There are only two real positives:  there is an air vent for each seat and the screen is large.  Taken together, I would never fly ANA again with this seat.

author: @Aviation.4.life5
text: Airbus manufactures the A320, a popular narrow-body, twin-engine jet airliner designed for short- to medium-haul routes. Introduced in 1988, it was the first commercial aircraft to feature a fully digital fly-by-wire control system, enhancing safety and efficiency. Typically seating 140 to 180 passengers, the A320 offers a range of about 3,300 nautical miles and is powered by either CFM or IAE engines, with newer A320neo models offering improved fuel efficiency. Known for its reliability, comfort, and advanced technology, the A320 remains a favorite among airlines worldwide and is one of the best-selling aircraft of all time.

author: @janeb2382
text:
Air NZ he Worst Business class !  Its old and grubby ! The new business class that Air NZ is rolling out soon just seems to be taking forever !!! They really need to catch up to the other Airlines products that are bench mark in this category. Oh and the insulting thing is that Air NZ is one of the most expensive $$$!!! Look at the costs of international flights to USA !!! Even business class to Australia return from Auckland is often over $2000. Compare !!! 

If travelling with friend or partner your better off in premium economy with normal seating arrangement and with all the same meals and frills as business class... You can also look out the window without breaking your neck,,, And a lot bit cheaper.

However, I feel the food is average if Im honest....Almost microwave tv meals, And a couple of slices of cheese with Huntley Palmers crackers... hardly premium in comparison to other airlines...And the annoying thing about that is the three choices you do have is the same on nearly all their other flights. So a bit ground hog day...  Ive  travelled many many times (monthly, often twice monthly) in business class on Air NZ out of Auckland and also other airlines such as Qatar. Emirates, Qantas... I just hope Air NZ premium classes rise to a higher standard when they update to the new cabins soon... no doubt the prices will also rise !!! Lets see...

text: I absolutely agree with you - I frequently fly Business Class SFO-SIN on Singapore airlines . My real complaint are that the beds narrow T the knees - think mummy bag. Unlikely Lufthansa , United or Turkish where business class is a better for taller people . I find the SA business class just feels like I can’t bend my k res when I sleep. Also - the food has slowly deteriorated but I like being able to order additional dishes beforehand. United in this route during my last two flights was abysmal - food, service and if you miss a meal and inquire afterwards it’s granola bars- That’s it. These are the only nonstop flights. I may have to consider a new hub.

author: @EKrishna-v9c
text: Singapore Airlines A380 and 787-10 are both very comfortable, yes you have to get help to get the lie flat bed sorted out and your feet do get wedged into a box but still all round value is ok. Qsuites are the way to go, we went Auckland New Zealand to Doha Qatar then Heathrow England on the A350 1000 and these are by far the best value, $9k USD for three seats, each as its own suite, with everything on demand, lovely 17hr 55 min flight to Doha and then 8hrs 35 mins to Heathrow, best all round experience. Stay clear of Herringbone seats, Air NZ is a real let down as the airline itself is very safe but their business class is just not comfortable, you want to lie down all the time just so as not to see your neighbors, but once lying down it is ok, but you can’t lie down forever. 😅

video_title: 20 HOUR Singapore Airlines BUSINESS CLASS FOOD REVIEW! - LA to Singapore
author: @adriansim5940
text:
Honestly, why does one need all that wasteful opulence and luxury? I used to fly business in the 90s and early 2000s. They were pretty much comfortable then. There were no flat beds, no pjs on offer etc etc etc. The airline lounge was well stocked with food and drinks which is pretty decent.  Then they started heaping all these wasteful luxury to keep up with the joneses...at the expense of the people at the back which btw makes up 95% of passenger volumes!!  I mean...who needs a suite while travelling?  Who needs to have a shower at 30,000ft?? Who needs a double bed while on business? Come on!! Except maybe to impress your friends on socials. Or those with large bank accounts who, btw, travel on private corporate jets anyway.

SIA's service at the back has deteriorated markedly...smaller meal trays ie smaller food portions. Smaller seats, less space all to heap wasteful luxuries on less than 10% of your passengers. If you were to remove SIA's business and first class products, you're left with only an expensive low cost airline!!

Hopefully, airlines come to realise that the 90% of passengers are important and spread the cash around the cabin rather than just up front just to fill up the social media pages....absolute garbage!!

Right now, there are much better offerings on the market without the snob appeal....EVA Air, Japan Airlines, Asiana, Cathay Pacific.

video_title: 20 HOUR Singapore Airlines BUSINESS CLASS FOOD REVIEW! - LA to Singapore
author: @adriansim5940
text: Mate, you can buy better breadrolls at your local bakery without having to spend $$$$ just to fly business and get a person to serve you warm bread in a basket on white china. Even a 5 Michelin starred restaurant doesn't cost that much lah. Get a life man!!!

video_title: 20 HOUR Singapore Airlines BUSINESS CLASS FOOD REVIEW! - LA to Singapore
author: @Ksandur

video_title: 20 HOUR Singapore Airlines BUSINESS CLASS FOOD REVIEW! - LA to Singapore
author: @Ksandur
text: Airline economy is (unfortunately) realizing that premium cabines - despite being only 10% of the passenger count - account for 90% of the cabin revenue. Economy passengers don't account for more than 10%, and as such their priority lies with Premium and Business. A long-haul low-cost airline has yet to be proven profitable with only economy seats. See; scoot with ScootPlus (ScootBiz) or Zipair with "Lie-flat". Economy only cabins on the longhaul just don't make money.

author: @heyyywhynot
text: Now, videos like these are always helpful, and don't get me wrong --- I'm a fan of AvGeekdom. But here's the one thing: why is such an essential component of AvGeek content the need to act like you're reviewing food like Gordon Ramsay? You're flying to a spectacular food city. Also, it's business, not first: you're not gonna be drinking Dom, nor do you need it. Your taste buds wouldn't appreciate Wagyu or filet of sole. Yes it would be great if there was one signature dish on the plane, but the business highlight for me is the opportunity to sleeeeep and with Singapore exactly a 12 hour difference, it's not that hard to calculate that your plane's taking off at 9:45 at night singapore time. If you can muster up the ability to pull an all nighter the night before and say, "Hello -- I'd like to go to sleep" as soon as you get on the plane, you're in better shape to conquer jetlag than you may think.

author: @Frank_E_Scialdone
text: I really enjoyed this video. I flew business class for the first time in my life. I've been living in Guangzhou China for many years. And traveling back to the states is such a long trip. And me being a larger person economy seats are just uncomfortable, not only for me but sometimes for other people. Anyway… I decided for the first time I was going to fly business class. And leaving from Guangzhou China was Japan Airlines. My first flight from China to Tokyo was a Business class seat.... that was very comfortable. It wasn't the modern one shown in the video, but still accommodating for a four hour trip… I then had a layover of 17 hours in the airport. But having access to the business class lounge was incredible. And then from Japan to JFK I did fly on the airline that was in this video and it was the 300 series. And that was an amazing seat. Literally so private with doors that went up. It was a great experience. I don't believe I'm ever going to fly coach ever again… The price is more expensive. But you get what you pay for. And traveling almost 35 hours. It's well worth the money to treat yourself.

text: Thank you Sam, this review has been exceptional for me.  I have a 30yo spinal injury & in constant severe pain.  I have travelled the world but never flown business but would like to cross Canada off my bucket list (from Australia) so found you doing research as to ‘what is a flat bed’?  This will be the only way for me to get there now.  It blows my mind to think I could pay $13,000 + for a flat bed seat and not really be flat or comfortable.  So this review has really opened my mind.  I am sharing with all my friends & also the travel company I do overseas trips with.  Hopefully I’ll get to Canada in May 2026.  Thank you so much, I am going to go through your other reviews now.

author: @sandippatel1969
text: @tradingplaces787 Currently recovering from an incredible night at Wembley seeing Oasis, mate. Last time I flew 1st was back in 2008 when I went to Mumbai. I had mentioned this to a patient the night before my flight, who unbeknownst to me, was quite high up at BA . Lo and behold I found myself in the Concorde room before boarding into a 2008 version of 1st class which was not at all what it is like these days. Quite a jump up from Premium Economy (a product which hasn't changed much at all).  These days, I keep an eye out on the upgrade prices (for example I am to Chicago this autumn in Club) and see whether it is worth it. Not sure it is worth it in BA as the difference isn't that much , but will definitely do so with ME and Asian carriers. I am accruing lots of Avios , but will have to switch allegiance as the ME carriers look much better. Emirates back in 2013 was a brilliant product and it has improved and I want to try the Qatar product. Keep up the fantastic work, mate. You are the best reviewer out there

author: @destineeryan6773
text: This precisely. My fiance is a person of logic and efficiency, and I'm very much of emotion and the little things. The other day we went to the grocery store, and its mascot is a Pig, which is my favorite animal. I was getting out our store loyalty card, and he said "You know we can just give them your phone number to get the points," and I said "Yeah, but then I wouldn't get to use my store card and see the cute little pig on it!" And his reaction was "You're so lovely, you remind me to enjoy the little things." Then we had a discussion about how we balance each other out, the kite and the line. He keeps me tethered and helps improve my efficiency and is able to show me a logical option I missed, I help him fly and remember that emotions, especially joy, even little pieces of it, make life much more fun. He keeps me from flying away, and I keep him from crashing down to the ground.

author: @B.-wy8rx
text:
I remember watching a doc about those doors both on Airbus and Boeing commercial planes, you have to pull them inside, before opening them. That means, with the differential pressure between cabin interior, and outside at high altitude (when explosive decompression can be fatal), you cannot open them (with an average adult force). You're just disengaging the lever, but that's it, it won't bulge further.
Nonetheless, this guy deserves a lengthy jail time.

author: @majorcalvary6515
text: I don’t necessarily like the lie flat seats. When I used to travel quite a bit on business class in late 90s and early 2000s.   Cathay Pacific, American, Japan Airlines, EVA Air: all pretty much had the same seats.  747-400 and 777-200 pretty much are the same seats. A340 was a little different. Once I was on a Japan Asia Airways, the business class was the old first class on a 747-200.  I’m a muscular guy, the seat itself might be ok even economy class for me, but should room tends to be problematic for me.  The old business class seat may not able to lie flat, it still had more shoulder room.  There were leg rests that went high enough; I still slept very well.

Like to mention, EVA new business class seat is not bad, not as good as ANA, but I actually prefer it more than SQ.
